Accessing SOT data — discover and use marine observation data

From real-time streams to climate archives — open access to ship-based data

Ship-based observations collected through the SOT programmes are made freely and openly available to the global community. Data is accessible in two main forms: (a) real-time data — for operational forecasting and immediate applications; or (b) delayed-mode data — for research, reanalysis, and long-term climate studies. These datasets are distributed through international systems and specialized data centres.

Real-Time Data Access: GTS and WIS2.0

SOT observations are distributed globally in near real-time via the Global Telecommunication System (GTS), or the emerging WIS2.0 infrastructure.

This real-time data includes VOS surface observations, ASAP upper-air soundings, and XBT SOOP profiles (where available in real time).

Users typically access this data through the National Meteorological Services, operational forecasting centers or WIS2.0 nodes and data services.

Delayed-mode data — GDACs

Delayed-mode processing allows for quality control procedures that are not possible in real time: comparison with climatological baselines, visual inspection of suspect observations, and correction of instrument calibration offsets identified after the fact. The result is a higher-quality dataset that forms the backbone of long-term climate records.

Ship observations in the Marine Climatological Summaries Scheme (MCSS) extend back to the mid-nineteenth century, making them among the longest continuous in-situ climate records in existence. The International Comprehensive Ocean-Atmosphere Data Set (ICOADS) — the world's primary historical marine climate archive — is built substantially on delayed-mode ship observations submitted through the MCSS.

Global Data Assembly Centres (GDACs) collect, quality control, archive, and distribute delayed-mode ocean and meteorological data. Compared to real-time data, delayed-mode datasets undergo enhanced quality control, include additional metadata, and optimized for research and climate applications.

Upper ocean thermal data from SOOP/XBT deployments are archived through the Global Temperature-Salinity Profile Programme (GTSPP) and through the Coriolis GDAC (France) and the US National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI). ASAP upper-air data are archived through the ECMWF and national meteorological service archives.

Open access marine observation data

  • Coriolis GDAC →
  • NCEI Marine Data →
  • Global Temperature and Salinity Profile Programme (GTSPP) →
  • VOS, drifting and moored buoys dataset International Comprehensive Ocean-Atmosphere Data Set (ICOADS) →

Scientific studies and projects

  • Fluxes from in-situ observations including VOS Florida State University →
  • THORPEX Experiment Global Modeling and Assimilation Office, NASA, USA →
  • VOSClim datasets VOS CLimate Project →
  • VOS, buoys and satellite datasets UK Met Office Hadley Centre →

Related articles

About the SOT

The Ship Observations Team coordinates global ship-based ocean and atmospheric observations — sustaining a record that has served science, safety, and climate understanding for more than 170 years.
